text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Approach to the work

Before drafting, work the matter methodically. These steps apply to any
legal deliverable; adapt the emphasis to the task.

1. **Read every document fully, then fix the scope.** `read` each file in
   `$DOCUMENTS_DIR` completely — decisive facts hide in late schedules,
   exhibits, defined-term sections, and worked tables, not just the
   narrative. Then determine, from the task's framing plus the operative
   transactional/instruction documents, **exactly which matter, party, or
   property is at issue and which documents bear on it.** When a set mixes
   documents for more than one matter, identify the right one deliberately;
   do **not** discard a document as "out of scope" unless you are sure —
   wrongly scoping out the relevant records discards the whole answer.

2. **For review / "identify issues" / audit / diligence tasks, build a
   complete issue census — this is usually where credit is won or lost.**
   Go through the operative document **section by section**, and each
   supporting document **one by one**, so coverage is exhaustive rather than
   limited to the first few obvious problems.
   - **Cross-reference every document against every other relevant document.**
     Many issues exist only in the *gap between two documents* and are invisible
     when a document is read alone: a contract term that violates a governing
     **policy, standard, statute, guideline, or requirements document** in the
     set (e.g. a duration or amount that exceeds an internal cap); a fact,
     date, title, or figure in one record that is inconsistent with what
     another record requires; a representation contradicted by a schedule;
     a required provision that is **absent**. Check the operative document
     against each policy/standard/requirements/counterparty document present.
   - **Surface every distinct issue you find**, not just the most salient —
     completeness across the full set of issues beats depth on a few.

3. **Develop each issue completely.** For every issue give, as the task and
   any output format require: (a) a precise identification grounded in a
   citation to the source record; (b) why it matters — the risk or
   consequence under the controlling standard or instruction; (c) a concrete
   recommendation or remediation; and (d) any classification/severity the
   format calls for. Partial treatment of an issue tends to lose credit.

4. **Stay grounded — do not invent.** Raise only issues supported by the
   record; never fabricate an issue, citation, or figure to fill out a
   template. If something required cannot be found, say so as a data gap
   rather than inventing it. Quote or cite figures, dates, and defined terms
   exactly as they appear.

## File formats

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):

{"file_path": "gap-analysis-memo.md", "content": "# MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT / ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION**

| | |
|---|---|
| **TO:** | Craig Halloran, Esq.; Whitmore & Callahan LLP PERM/I-140 Engagement Team; File |
| **FROM:** | Immigration Case Review |
| **DATE:** | June 9, 2025 |
| **RE:** | Credential Gap Analysis — PERM Application (ETA-9089), Case No. A-18247-63910 — *Prismex Analytics, Inc.* v. Dr. Ananya Mehrotra — Position: Senior Machine Learning Engineer |

---

## I. Introduction and Scope

This memorandum reviews the certified PERM Application for Permanent Employment Certification (ETA Form 9089), Case No. **A-18247-63910** (filed March 15, 2025; certified May 22, 2025), filed by **Prismex Analytics, Inc.** on behalf of beneficiary **Dr. Ananya Mehrotra** for the position of **Senior Machine Learning Engineer** (SOC 15-2051, Data Scientists). The purpose of this review is to test, requirement by requirement, whether the beneficiary's supporting credentials actually satisfy the minimum requirements attested on the PERM application, to identify every gap or inconsistency between the application and the supporting record, and to provide concrete remedial recommendations.

A threshold point of framing: **PERM certification does not constitute a DOL finding that the beneficiary is qualified.** Certification reflects the employer's attestations and compliant recruitment; DOL does not independently verify the beneficiary's education, experience, or special skills at the certification stage. The beneficiary's qualifications are scrutinized independently by USCIS at the I-140 stage, and may be re-examined by DOL upon audit or in a revocation proceeding. Accordingly, any gap between the PERM attestations (Section K) and the supporting credentials is a live risk that must be cured before the I-140 is filed — not a problem that certification has already resolved.

The headline finding is that the PERM application attests (Section K.3) that the beneficiary possesses all special skills and other requirements, including an **"AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or equivalent,"** but the beneficiary's own certification record — compiled by this firm — confirms she **does not hold that certification**, and the only AWS credential she does hold **expired before the priority date**. This is a material qualification gap that must be addressed before the I-140.

---

## II. Documents Reviewed

1. **PERM Application (ETA-9089)** — Case No. A-18247-63910, filed March 15, 2025, certified May 22, 2025 (`perm-eta-9089.docx`).
2. **Beneficiary Resume / CV** — Dr. Ananya Mehrotra (`mehrotra-resume.docx`).
3. **Professional Certifications Compilation** — prepared by Whitmore & Callahan LLP, June 2025 (`certifications-compiled.docx`).
4. **Credential Evaluation Report** — International Academic Evaluators (IAE), Report No. IAE-2025-02-4738, dated February 15, 2025 (`iae-credential-evaluation.docx`).
5. **NC State University Official Transcript** — M.S. in Computer Science, issued May 20, 2025 (`ncstate-transcript.docx`).
6. **DataBridge Solutions LLC Experience Letter** — Dr. Samuel Okonkwo, VP of Data Science, dated April 10, 2025 (`databridge-experience-letter.docx`).
7. **Evalpoint Technologies Pvt. Ltd. Experience Letter** — Vikram Reddy, Director of Engineering, dated March 28, 2025 (`evalpoint-experience-letter.docx`).
8. **Prevailing Wage Determination (PWD)** — Tracking No. P-200-24187-329156, issued November 8, 2024 (`prevailing-wage-determination.docx`).
9. **HR-to-Counsel Email** — Melanie Foss (HR Director, Prismex) to Craig Halloran, dated June 4, 2025 (`hr-to-counsel-email.eml`).

---

## III. PERM Minimum Requirements (As Attested)

The following minimum requirements are stated on the PERM application (Section J.B) and are the standard against which the beneficiary's credentials are measured:

- **Education (J.B.1):** "Master's degree in Computer Science, Machine Learning, or a closely related field." Foreign educational equivalent accepted.
- **Experience (J.B.3):** "5 years of progressive post-master's experience in machine learning engineering or a closely related occupation."
- **Special Skills / Other Requirements (J.B.5):**
  - (a) Designing and deploying production-grade deep learning models using **TensorFlow or PyTorch**;
  - (b) Experience with distributed computing frameworks including **Apache Spark**;
  - (c) **Natural language processing (NLP)** pipeline development;
  - (d) Cloud-based ML deployment on **AWS SageMaker or Google Vertex AI**;
  - (e) Proficiency in **Python and R** programming languages;
  - **AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or equivalent required**;
  - Up to 10% domestic travel;
  - Will supervise a team of 3–5 ML engineers.
- **No alternate combination of education and experience is accepted (J.B.4).**

The employer attested in Section K that the beneficiary meets the education requirement (K.1 = Yes), the experience requirement (K.2 = Yes), and the special skills/other requirements (K.3 = Yes), and is qualified (K.4 = Yes), and that no qualifying experience was gained with the petitioning employer (K.5 = No).

---

## IV. Summary of the Beneficiary's Documented Credentials

**Education.** M.S. in Computer Science, North Carolina State University, conferred **May 12, 2018** (GPA 3.74/4.00; transcript). Prior degree: B.Tech in Electronics and Communication Engineering, Maharashtra Institute of Technology, Pune (Savitribai Phule Pune University), conferred **June 2013**, evaluated by IAE (NACES member) as equivalent to a U.S. bachelor's degree in Electronics and Communication Engineering.

**Post-Master's Experience.** DataBridge Solutions LLC — Data Scientist I (June 4, 2018 – January 3, 2021), promoted to Data Scientist II (January 4, 2021 – present), full-time, 40 hrs/week, supervisor Dr. Samuel Okonkwo (VP, Data Science). Confirmed by experience letter dated April 10, 2025. This is the sole source of the qualifying post-master's experience (~6.75 years as of the March 15, 2025 priority date; ~7 years as of the May 22, 2025 certification).

**Pre-Master's Experience (background only; does not count toward the 5-year post-master's requirement).** Evalpoint Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Software Engineer (July 1, 2013 – July 15, 2016), Bangalore, India. NC State Department of Computer Science, Research Assistant (August 15, 2016 – May 15, 2018, 20 hrs/week), supervisor Professor Helen Tsai, funded under NSF Grant No. IIS-1742956.

**Certifications.** (i) AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner — Foundational level, issued January 18, 2022, **expired January 18, 2025**; (ii) TensorFlow Developer Certificate — issued March 10, 2023, no expiration, active (issued by Google). Per the certifications compilation (Verification Note 3), counsel specifically inquired and the beneficiary **confirmed she does not hold the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or any AWS specialty-level certification**.

---

## V. Gap Analysis

The table below summarizes the requirement-by-requirement comparison. Detailed treatment of each gap follows.

| # | Requirement / Item (PERM cite) | PERM Attestation | Documented Credential | Status | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AWS Certified ML – Specialty cert "or equivalent" (J.B.5) | K.3 = Yes | Not held; beneficiary confirmed she does not hold it | **GAP** | **Critical** |
| 2 | Active AWS credential at priority date | K.3 = Yes | AWS Cloud Practitioner expired Jan 18, 2025 (before March 15, 2025 priority date) | **GAP** | **High** |
| 3 | K.3 attestation vs. certifications compilation | K.3 = Yes | Counsel's compilation confirms cert not held | **Inconsistency** | **High** |
| 4 | FEIN consistency (PERM C.7 vs. PWD) | PERM: 84-3291756 | PWD: 84-3291047 | **Inconsistency** | **Moderate** |
| 5 | NC State RA experience verification letter | Listed as Experience Entry 2 (J.C) | No letter; only transcript (HR email flags gap) | **Doc gap** | **Moderate** |
| 6 | Supervisory scope (3–5 ML engineers) (J.B.5) | "Will supervise 3–5" | Prior experience supervised 2 junior data scientists | Observation | **Low** |
| 7 | R proficiency (J.B.5(e)) | K.3 = Yes | Coursework (ST 558, A-) + "some use" / "intermediate" | Thin | **Low** |
| 8 | "Approx. 7 years" post-master's (K.2) | ~7 years | ~6.75 years as of priority date | Immaterial | **Low** |
| 9 | Education — Master's in CS/ML/related (J.B.1) | K.1 = Yes | M.S. CS, NC State, May 2018 | **Satisfied** | — |
| 10 | Experience — 5 yrs progressive post-master's (J.B.3) | K.2 = Yes | DataBridge June 2018–present (~6.75 yrs) | **Satisfied** | — |
| 11 | Special skills (a)–(d): TensorFlow/PyTorch, Spark, NLP, SageMaker (J.B.5) | K.3 = Yes | All documented in DataBridge DS II role | **Satisfied** | — |
| 12 | Python proficiency (J.B.5(e)) | K.3 = Yes | Expert / primary language | **Satisfied** | — |

### Gap 1 — AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification: required but not held (CRITICAL)

**Identification.** Section J.B.5 expressly requires the "AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or equivalent." Section K.3 attests "Yes," the beneficiary possesses the special skills and other requirements. The certifications compilation (Verification Note 3) states: "Counsel specifically inquired whether the beneficiary holds the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification or any other AWS specialty-level certification; the beneficiary confirmed she does not hold any such certification." The beneficiary's only AWS credential is the AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner, which the compilation describes as a "foundational-level certification [that] does not demonstrate specialization in any specific AWS service domain, including machine learning." Her other credential, the TensorFlow Developer Certificate, is "issued by Google and is not affiliated with Amazon Web Services (AWS)."

**Why it matters.** The certification is a stated minimum requirement, not a job duty. At the I-140 stage, USCIS independently determines whether the beneficiary possessed each minimum requirement — including special-skills certifications — **as of the priority date** (March 15, 2025). A beneficiary who cannot be shown to have held a required certification as of the priority date is exposed to an I-140 denial for failure to establish she meets the minimum requirements, and the approved labor certification is exposed to a DOL audit denial or revocation proceeding on the same basis. The gap also creates a dual risk: (i) if the requirement is bona fide, the beneficiary is not qualified; (ii) if the requirement was not actually applied by the employer, the PERM's bona fides and the recruitment's validity are themselves questionable (a requirement stated on the form but not actually enforced is a classic audit vulnerability). The "or equivalent" language does not cure the gap: neither the AWS Cloud Practitioner (foundational, AWS-branded but not ML-specialty) nor the TensorFlow Developer Certificate (ML-relevant but Google-issued and framework-specific, not an AWS specialty credential) is a functional equivalent of the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification.

**Severity.** Critical.

**Recommendation.**
1. **Immediately schedule the beneficiary to sit for and obtain the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification.** This is the only credential that unambiguously satisfies the requirement.
2. **Assess the "as-of-priority-date" problem with counsel.** Because the certification was not held on March 15, 2025, a certification obtained now post-dates the priority date and may not, by itself, establish qualification as of that date. Counsel must evaluate whether (a) the beneficiary can demonstrate she possessed the underlying AWS ML competencies as of the priority date and the certification merely formalizes them (a fact-intensive argument that USCIS may reject for a *certification* requirement, as distinct from a *skill* requirement), or (b) the safer course is to **refile the PERM** after the beneficiary obtains the certification, so that the certification predates the new priority date. Refiling forfeits the current priority date and restarts recruitment, which is costly given the STEM OPT deadline (see Section IX) — but it may be the only defensible path if the requirement is bona fide.
3. **Re-examine whether the certification was a bona fide minimum requirement.** If the employer did not in fact require the AWS ML – Specialty certification for the position, the requirement should not have been listed, and the PERM is exposed on bona-fides grounds. If it was not required, counsel should weigh refiling without the certification requirement against the risk of proceeding on a certification the beneficiary lacks. Do **not** simply proceed on the current PERM while representing to USCIS that the beneficiary meets a requirement the record shows she does not.

### Gap 2 — AWS Cloud Practitioner expired before the priority date (HIGH)

**Identification.** The AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner was issued January 18, 2022 and **expired January 18, 2025** — approximately two months **before** the March 15, 2025 priority date. The certifications compilation confirms the status is "EXPIRED" and that "Dr. Mehrotra has not renewed this certification."

**Why it matters.** Even setting aside that the Cloud Practitioner is foundational and not equivalent to the required ML – Specialty certification (Gap 1), the beneficiary held **no active AWS certification of any kind as of the priority date**. This eliminates any fallback argument that an active (if lesser) AWS credential satisfied the "or equivalent" language, and it means the record contains no AWS credential that was valid on the date the employer attested (K.3) that the beneficiary met the AWS certification requirement. This compounds Gap 1 and sharpens the misattestation concern.

**Severity.** High (compounds Gap 1).

**Recommendation.** Treat as part of the Gap 1 remediation. If the beneficiary renews/obtains AWS credentials, ensure the target is the **ML – Specialty** certification (the required credential), not merely a renewal of the Cloud Practitioner. Document the issuance date of any new credential and analyze the priority-date implications as in Gap 1.

### Gap 3 — Section K.3 attestation contradicted by counsel's own certifications compilation (HIGH)

**Identification.** Section K.3 attests "Yes" — the beneficiary possesses the special skills and other requirements of J.B.5, which include the AWS ML – Specialty certification. The certifications compilation prepared by this firm (Verification Note 3) affirmatively documents that the beneficiary does **not** hold that certification. The two records are internally inconsistent.

**Why it matters.** This is a material inconsistency within the engagement file. The K.3 attestation was made under penalty of perjury (Section L alien declaration; Section N employer certification). A K.3 attestation that the beneficiary meets a requirement the file shows she does not meet creates exposure to a finding of misattestation, which can support denial, revocation, and referral for investigation. It also means the firm cannot, in good faith, file an I-140 that relies on K.3 without first reconciling the record.

**Severity.** High.

**Recommendation.** Reconcile the inconsistency before any I-140 filing. Either (a) cure the underlying gap per Gaps 1–2 and confirm the beneficiary now holds the required certification (with priority-date analysis), or (b) if the requirement is being withdrawn as non-bona-fide, refile the PERM with corrected J.B.5/K.3 attestations. Do not file the I-140 on the current record. Document the reconciliation in the file.

### Gap 4 — FEIN discrepancy between the PERM application and the PWD (MODERATE)

**Identification.** The PERM application (Section C.7) lists the employer's Federal Employer Identification Number as **84-3291756**. The Prevailing Wage Determination (Section 1) lists the FEIN as **84-3291047**. The two numbers differ. All other employer identifiers (legal name "Prismex Analytics, Inc.," address 4500 Mueller Blvd, Suite 300, Austin, TX 78723, POC Melanie Foss, counsel Craig Halloran) are consistent across the two documents.

**Why it matters.** The PWD must correspond to the petitioning employer. A FEIN mismatch between the PWD and the PERM can raise a question whether the wage determination applies to the entity that filed the PERM, and USCIS cross-checks employer identity and ability-to-pay at the I-140 stage using the FEIN. If the PERM contains a transposed or erroneous FEIN, the error should be identified and the correct FEIN documented now so that the I-140, I-129, and ability-to-pay evidence all use a single, correct, consistent FEIN. If the two FEINs reflect two different entities (e.g., a parent/subsidiary or predecessor), that is a more serious issue requiring analysis of which entity is the lawful employer/petitioner.

**Severity.** Moderate.

**Recommendation.** Confirm the correct FEIN against Prismex's tax/payroll records (payroll is administered by Cornerpoint Payroll Services per the PERM addendum and HR email). Determine which document contains the error. Ensure the I-140, I-129, and all ability-to-pay documentation use the correct FEIN consistent with the employer's IRS records. If the PERM's FEIN is wrong, retain documentation of the correct FEIN and be prepared to address the discrepancy if raised by DOL or USCIS; if the discrepancy reflects an entity issue, escalate to counsel immediately.

### Gap 5 — Missing experience verification letter for the NC State Research Assistant position (MODERATE)

**Identification.** The PERM lists the NC State Research Assistant appointment (August 15, 2016 – May 15, 2018, 20 hrs/week) as Experience Entry 2 (Section J.C). The HR email (June 4, 2025) expressly flags that "the package does not include an experience verification letter from North Carolina State University for Dr. Mehrotra's Research Assistant position," that Professor Helen Tsai (the supervisor) "is apparently on sabbatical and has not responded to emails," and that the team "figured the transcript should suffice." The transcript confirms the RA appointment, period, supervisor, and funding source, but it does not describe the duties performed in the detail DOL expects in an experience verification.

**Why it matters.** In a PERM audit, DOL expects each claimed period of experience to be documented, preferably by a letter from the former employer/supervisor on letterhead describing the dates, hours, and duties. A transcript is corroborative but is not a substitute for an experience letter. That said, the exposure is limited here because the RA position is **pre-master's-degree-completion** (it was held during the M.S. program, which was conferred May 12, 2018) and therefore **does not count toward the 5-year post-master's requirement**, which is satisfied entirely by the DataBridge experience (Gap analysis items 10–11). The RA entry is background/supplementary. Nonetheless, because it is a listed experience entry, an audit could request verification of it, and the RA experience is also the earliest documented source of the beneficiary's PyTorch/NLP work.

**Severity.** Moderate (lowered by the fact that the RA experience is not part of the qualifying 5 years).

**Recommendation.** Obtain a written verification of the RA appointment from NC State. Because Professor Tsai is on sabbatical, pursue the Department of Computer Science administrator and/or the NC State University HR/Sponsored Programs office (the appointment was funded under NSF Grant No. IIS-1742956, which creates an administrative record). The letter should confirm dates, hours/week, the RA title, the supervisor, and a description of duties consistent with the resume and PERM (NLP research, transformer-based text classification in PyTorch, clinical dataset preprocessing, co-authored papers). If a letter cannot be obtained, supplement the file with the transcript, the resume, the two publications (which name Professor Tsai as co-author/advisor), and a detailed beneficiary declaration describing the RA duties, and be prepared to explain the unavailability of a supervisor letter in any audit response.

### Gap 6 — Supervisory scope: prior experience (2) vs. offered role (3–5) (LOW)

**Identification.** J.B.5 states the position "Will supervise a team of 3–5 ML engineers." The DataBridge experience letter and the resume both describe the beneficiary as having "Led a team of 2 junior data scientists" in the Data Scientist II role.

**Why it matters.** The "Will supervise" language denotes a future job duty of the offered position, not a minimum prior-experience requirement, so the beneficiary's prior supervision of 2 (rather than 3–5) is not, on the face of the form, a qualification gap. The risk is one of construction: if USCIS or DOL were to read supervisory responsibility over 3–5 engineers as a minimum special skill, the beneficiary's prior experience (supervising 2) would not match. The more natural reading is that supervision is a job duty, in which case there is no gap.

**Severity.** Low.

**Recommendation.** Confirm that supervision of 3–5 ML engineers is intended as a job duty of the offered position (consistent with the "Will supervise" phrasing) and not a minimum prior-experience requirement. If the latter was intended, the requirement should be reconciled with the beneficiary's actual prior supervisory experience. No change is needed if it is a job duty; consider whether the recruitment materials and job order consistently frame it as a duty.

### Gap 7 — R proficiency is documented but thin (LOW)

**Identification.** J.B.5(e) requires "proficiency in Python and R programming languages." Python is strongly documented (resume: "expert"; DataBridge letter: "primary programming language"). R is supported by (i) coursework — ST 558 "Statistical Computing with R," grade A- (transcript); and (ii) professional use — the DataBridge letter describes "some use of R for statistical reporting and ad hoc data analysis," and the resume lists R as "intermediate."

**Why it matters.** "Proficiency" is a relatively modest standard, and the combination of formal coursework (A-) plus professional use likely satisfies it. However, the professional-use evidence is comparatively thin ("some use," "intermediate"), so the R requirement is the least robustly documented of the technical skills. In an audit or RFE, the employer should be prepared to substantiate R proficiency beyond the single coursework line.

**Severity.** Low.

**Recommendation.** No immediate action required, but (a) consider asking DataBridge to expand the experience letter's description of R usage (e.g., specific statistical reporting deliverables, packages used), and (b) ensure the beneficiary's resume and any I-140 support letter consistently reflect R usage. Be prepared to point to the ST 558 coursework and the DataBridge R usage if questioned.

### Gap 8 — "Approximately 7 years" post-master's experience slightly overstated as of the priority date (LOW)

**Identification.** Section K.2 states the beneficiary has "approximately 7 years of post-master's experience at DataBridge Solutions LLC (June 2018 – present)." The M.S. was conferred May 12, 2018; DataBridge employment began June 4, 2018. Measured to the March 15, 2025 priority date, the period is approximately **6 years, 9 months** (~6.75 years); measured to the May 22, 2025 certification date, it is approximately 6 years, 11.5 months (~7 years).

**Why it matters.** The "approximately 7 years" figure is a rounding that is slightly generous as of the priority date but is immaterial because the beneficiary exceeds the 5-year minimum by a comfortable margin in any event. No qualification gap results.

**Severity.** Low (informational).

**Recommendation.** No action required. If the I-140 support letter or any filing recites the experience duration, use a precise figure (e.g., "approximately 6 years and 9 months as of the priority date, and continuing") rather than "7 years," to avoid any appearance of overstatement.

### Items satisfied (no gap)

- **Education (J.B.1 / K.1):** Satisfied. M.S. in Computer Science, NC State, conferred May 12, 2018 (transcript; PERM J.A Degree 2; resume). The B.Tech in Electronics and Communication Engineering (MIT-Pune, June 2013), evaluated by IAE as equivalent to a U.S. bachelor's, is the prior/admission degree and is supporting; it is not required to satisfy the Master's minimum.
- **Experience quantity and progression (J.B.3 / K.2):** Satisfied. DataBridge Data Scientist I → Data Scientist II (June 4, 2018 – present) provides well over 5 years of progressive post-master's experience in a closely related occupation (Data Scientist ↔ Machine Learning Engineer, both within SOC 15-2051). The Evalpoint (2013–2016) and NC State RA (2016–2018) periods are pre-master's and are correctly excluded from the 5-year count.
- **Special skills (a)–(d) (J.B.5):** Satisfied by the DataBridge Data Scientist II role — deep learning with TensorFlow **and** PyTorch; Apache Spark (MLlib); NLP pipelines (NER, sentiment analysis, clinical document processing); AWS SageMaker deployment. The NC State RA additionally documents PyTorch-based NLP work.
- **Python proficiency (J.B.5(e)):** Satisfied (expert / primary language).
- **No qualifying experience with the petitioning employer (K.5):** Consistent — the beneficiary has never been employed by Prismex Analytics, Inc.; her current employer is DataBridge Solutions LLC.

---

## VI. Cross-Document Inconsistencies

1. **FEIN (Gap 4 above).** PERM C.7 (84-3291756) vs. PWD Section 1 (84-3291047). Reconcile before I-140/I-129.
2. **K.3 attestation vs. certifications compilation (Gap 3 above).** The PERM attests the beneficiary meets the AWS certification requirement; the firm's own compilation confirms she does not. Reconcile before I-140.
3. **Minor labeling items (informational, not substantive gaps):**
   - The IAE credential evaluation states its purpose as "For immigration purposes — U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS)" and covers only the B.Tech. This is appropriate for the I-140 stage; for the PERM/DOL context the evaluation is supporting only (the qualifying degree is the domestic M.S., which requires no evaluation). No correction needed, but the evaluation should not be presented as satisfying the PERM education requirement — the M.S. transcript does that.
   - The certifications compilation is captioned "Compiled for H-1B Petition Filing" (June 2025), consistent with the current H-1B/I-129 stage. Its contents are nonetheless the operative record of the beneficiary's certifications for purposes of this PERM credential review.

All other cross-document identity data are consistent: beneficiary name and DOB (March 14, 1991); passport (T4827361); SEVIS ID (N0003847261); I-94 (94-2023-08741256); address (2417 Hillsborough St, Apt 6C, Raleigh, NC 27607); M.S. conferral (May 12, 2018); B.Tech conferral (June 2013); DataBridge dates/titles/supervisor; Evalpoint dates/title/supervisor; RA dates/supervisor; PWD tracking number, SOC code, wage level, prevailing wage ($131,747), and validity period; offered wage ($142,000); employer name and address; counsel identity.

---

## VII. Documentation Gaps and Timing Observations

1. **Missing NC State RA experience letter (Gap 5 above).** Obtain from the department administrator or university HR/Sponsored Programs; supplement with transcript, resume, publications, and beneficiary declaration if a supervisor letter is unavailable.

2. **Supporting documents post-date the PERM filing (informational — not a gap).** The DataBridge letter (April 10, 2025), Evalpoint letter (March 28, 2025), and NC State transcript (issued May 20, 2025) all post-date the March 15, 2025 filing. This is permissible and routine for PERM: supporting documentation is retained in the audit file and need not accompany the electronic filing, and experience letters may be obtained after filing so long as they accurately describe experience gained before the priority date. The underlying facts (degrees conferred, employment periods) all pre-date the priority date. No action required; noted for completeness.

3. **Recruitment documentation (informational).** The PERM recites compliant recruitment (SWA job order; two Sunday print ads; and additional steps including company website, IEEE Computational Intelligence Magazine, and NC State campus recruitment), with three applicants, all found unqualified. The HR email confirms the full recruitment file is maintained by Broadleaf Staffing Advisors and available on request. This review did not examine the underlying recruitment evidence; counsel should confirm the recruitment file is complete and audit-ready (including the notice of filing, which the PERM lists at G.2.b as an "internal posting" — the worksite/in-house notice of filing is a separate 20 CFR 656.10(d) requirement and should not be counted as one of the three additional recruitment steps under 656.17(e)(1)(ii); the remaining three steps (company website, IEEE posting, campus recruitment) appear to satisfy the three-step minimum in any event).

---

## VIII. Prioritized Remedial Recommendations

**Priority 1 — Cure the AWS certification gap (Critical; Gaps 1–3).**
- Schedule the beneficiary to obtain the **AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty** certification immediately.
- Conduct a priority-date analysis with counsel: determine whether a post-priority-date certification can support the I-140 on the current PERM, or whether a **refiling** (with the certification predating the new priority date, or with the certification requirement removed if it was not bona fide) is the defensible course.
- Reconcile the K.3 attestation with the certifications compilation before any I-140 filing. Do not file the I-140 on the current unreconciled record.

**Priority 2 — Reconcile the FEIN (Moderate; Gap 4).**
- Confirm the correct FEIN with Prismex/Cornerpoint Payroll Services; identify which document (PERM or PWD) is in error; ensure the I-140, I-129, and ability-to-pay evidence use one correct, consistent FEIN; escalate if the discrepancy reflects an entity (not typographical) issue.

**Priority 3 — Obtain the NC State RA verification (Moderate; Gap 5).**
- Pursue the department administrator / university HR / Sponsored Programs office (NSF Grant No. IIS-1742956 record); supplement with transcript, resume, publications, and beneficiary declaration if a supervisor letter cannot be obtained.

**Priority 4 — Strengthen the R-proficiency record (Low; Gap 7).**
- Consider an expanded DataBridge letter describing R usage; ensure consistent representation across the resume and I-140 support letter.

**Priority 5 — Confirm the supervisory framing and recruitment file (Low; Gaps 6 and VII.3).**
- Confirm supervision of 3–5 ML engineers is a job duty (not a minimum prior-experience requirement); confirm the recruitment file is complete and audit-ready and that the notice of filing is not improperly counted as an additional recruitment step.

**Priority 6 — Precision in experience recitations (Low; Gap 8).**
- Use precise post-master's duration figures in the I-140 support letter and filings.

---

## IX. Timeline and Status Considerations

The HR email (June 4, 2025) indicates the employer intends to file the **H-1B (I-129)** petition — selected in the FY2026 cap lottery — by approximately **July 15, 2025**, and that the beneficiary's **STEM OPT expires September 15, 2025**. The employer has asked counsel to confirm cap-gap/status-continuation mechanics. Two points bear on the credential gaps:

1. **The H-1B (I-129) and the I-140 are distinct filings with distinct standards.** The credential gaps identified above (principally the AWS certification) bear most directly on the **I-140** (immigrant petition), where USCIS tests whether the beneficiary meets the PERM minimum requirements as of the priority date. The H-1B petition is governed by a "specialty occupation" standard and does not require the beneficiary to meet the PERM minimum requirements. Accordingly, the AWS certification gap is **not necessarily a blocker to filing the H-1B**, but it **is a blocker to filing a defensible I-140** on the current record. Counsel should keep these workstreams separate and not let the H-1B filing deadline obscure the I-140 remediation.

2. **The STEM OPT deadline constrains the refiling option.** If the priority-date analysis concludes that the AWS certification gap can only be cured by refiling the PERM (Gap 1, Recommendation 2(b)), the beneficiary's September 15, 2025 STEM OPT expiration and the need for ongoing employment authorization become material. Counsel should advise the employer and beneficiary on how a PERM refiling interacts with the H-1B cap-gap extension and any subsequent extension/bridge-petition strategy, and should sequence the filings accordingly. The cap-gap question posed by HR (whether F-1 STEM OPT and employment authorization extend through October 1 upon timely filing of the I-129) should be answered in a separate status-continuation advisory, as it is outside the scope of this credential review.

---

## X. Conclusion

The beneficiary's credentials satisfy the PERM **education** requirement (M.S. in Computer Science, NC State, 2018) and the **experience** requirement (well over 5 years of progressive post-master's experience at DataBridge Solutions LLC), as well as the great majority of the **special skills** (TensorFlow/PyTorch, Apache Spark, NLP, AWS SageMaker, Python). The record also contains a sound NACES credential evaluation of the foreign B.Tech and consistent identity data across all documents.

However, the record contains one **critical** gap and two **high**-severity issues that must be resolved before a defensible I-140 can be filed: the beneficiary **does not hold the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification** that Section J.B.5 makes a minimum requirement (and the only AWS credential she held **expired before the priority date**), and the Section K.3 attestation that she meets that requirement is **contradicted by this firm's own certifications compilation**. A **moderate** FEIN discrepancy between the PERM and the PWD and a **moderate** documentation gap (the missing NC State RA verification letter) should also be cured. Several **low**-severity items (supervisory scope, R-proficiency depth, experience-duration precision) warrant attention but do not, standing alone, defeat qualification.

Counsel should prioritize (1) curing the AWS certification gap and reconciling K.3, including a priority-date/refiling analysis; (2) reconciling the FEIN; and (3) obtaining the NC State RA verification — and should keep the H-1B (I-129) and I-140 workstreams on separate tracks given the differing legal standards and the STEM OPT deadline.

---

*Prepared for the engagement file. This memorandum is privileged and confidential and is intended solely for the use of the attorney engagement team and the employer in connection with the above-referenced matter.*"}
